artifacts.content:
Elena,

Kids are finally in bed and I’m staring at this stack of collision maps again. I keep thinking about how much of our life is wrapped up in these few streets.

Every time I bike Mateo to his Saturday soccer at the Tahoe Park fields, that stretch by the 7‑Eleven at 14th & Stockton still makes my stomach clench. I can’t get the March 2022 almost‑dooring out of my head—one car door a second earlier and I’d have been on the pavement with him watching. It’s part of why I pushed so hard for those buffered bike lanes on 14th. We’re still a one‑car household after everything with your schedule and Dad’s medical bills; that corridor is basically our family’s spine.

And then there’s all the kid stuff on top of it. Sofia still tenses up walking near 14th & 63rd after that March 8, 2023 near‑miss on her way to Tahoe Elementary. I know I tell people at work the project is about “neighborhood safety,” but inside it’s about *our* kids, our block at 4125 62nd, Mom’s fall at 60th, Luis Hernandez getting killed at 60th back in November 2021—this whole corridor is a scrapbook of close calls and one tragedy too many.

I also keep replaying that May 2, 2022 road‑rage incident at 14th & 58th when that driver screamed at you and swerved toward you and Mateo. No wonder you insist on walking him to Tahoe at 7:45 every morning.

Sorry, this got heavy. Tomorrow let’s just bike to Maple Park if you’re up for it after your shift and try to remember why we’re fighting so hard to fix these streets.

– D

metadata.subject: Why this corridor won’t leave my head

artifacts.content:
Mariana,

I’ve been meaning to write a real update instead of those quick texts.

First, your mom’s caldo on Sunday was exactly what we all needed. Mine’s been struggling more since her Type 2 diabetes diagnosis at Kaiser South Sacramento last January. She’s trying to be good about walking—30–40 minutes along 14th Ave to the Kaiser clinic three times a week—but I hate how stressed she looks at some of those crossings. She told me last week she almost turned around at 60th because the traffic felt too fast.

It’s wild how much of our family history is tied to busted concrete. Remember when she fell and fractured her wrist at the broken curb ramp at 14th & 60th back on 11/3/21, trying to get to the bus for that Kaiser appointment? That’s burned into my brain every time I review ADA ramp details. Same with Dad almost going down twice last winter stepping into my car in front of 4125 because of the ponding and broken pavement—this, after his knee surgery at Sutter in Feb 2021.

I know I sound like a walking CEQA memo, but honestly, the “minor drainage inlet adjustments” and curb extensions we’re doing are really about Mom and Dad and the other abuelos on our block. When I say the project is for “seniors and people with mobility issues,” I’m picturing Mom with her cane and glucose meter.

Anyway, enough planner ranting. How are you holding up with your shifts and Miguel’s recovery after his crash near Mercy General? Let’s plan a low‑key dinner at our place in a couple weeks—no tamales arms race between the moms this time, just something simple.

Un abrazo,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Mom, Dad, and all these sidewalks

artifacts.content:
Javi,

Catching you up on the not‑so‑Instagrammable parts of life.

Work: I finally got the 14th Ave safety project locked in as a Class 1 “existing facilities” rehab—pavement, striping, ADA ramps, lighting, all inside the current curb lines. After the City Attorney’s June 2022 closed‑session warning about how any perceived capacity increase could complicate Luis Herrera’s ADA lawsuit over his fall at the bad curb ramp by 62nd, I’ve been almost paranoid about staying within §15301(c).

That paranoia isn’t totally abstract either. I’m still carrying baggage from the 2019 internal audit that nailed me on the Broadway restriping hazmat oversight and landed that written PIP in my HR file through June 2020. Then there was the Franklin Blvd CEQA schedule I blew, the May 2023 performance plan, Karen Lee’s “we do not need an EIR on this one” talk in August, and Kevin Liu’s anonymous August 2023 complaint accusing me of rubber‑stamping exemptions while I was on FMLA for stress after Mateo’s behavioral suspension from Mark Twain Elementary. It’s…a lot.

On top of that, I met with the City Attorney, Lisa Hernandez, on Dec 12, 2024 about the whistleblower retaliation grievance I filed in October when a former deputy director tried to get me to “find a way” around an EIR on that infill project. I literally walked out of her office, went to my 3rd‑floor cubicle, and ran EnviroStor checks on every parcel in the new Class 1 corridors just so no one can say I cut corners.

Home is the only place that feels sane: Elena’s hanging in there after the license mess, Sofia’s obsessed with fractions, and Mateo’s back on the soccer field instead of the principal’s office. Come over for carne asada soon; I’ll trade you CEQA war stories for baby pictures.

– D

metadata.subject: Unfiltered work download (keep this between us)

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – why these projects feel so loaded lately

I keep telling people the 14th Ave work is “routine rehab,” but there’s nothing routine about how it sits in my gut.

I still see Sofia in that crosswalk at 14th & 58th on the morning of 9/14/23, when the car blew through and nearly sideswiped her. A year before that, Diego from down the block actually *was* hit there in May 2022 and ended up at UC Davis Med Center. Add Luis Hernandez’s fatal crash at 14th & 60th on 11/4/21, and it feels like every block of that corridor has a ghost.

Mom’s story is literally written into the concrete too: the fall and fractured wrist at the busted curb ramp at 14th & 60th on 11/3/21, then her diabetes diagnosis at Kaiser South Sac in Jan 2023 and those 30–40 minute walks along 14th to the clinic three times a week. Dad with his cane after the Sutter knee surgery in Feb 2021, almost slipping twice at the curb in front of 4125 because of ponding.

Then there’s our own choices: going down to one car in July 2022 after Dad’s stroke at Methodist and the $18k in medical/caregiving costs; Elena’s March 2023 DUI and six‑month license suspension that turned 14th Ave into our family’s lifeline on foot to Food Source and the clinic at Stockton. All of that is why buffered bike lanes, curb extensions, and crosswalks don’t feel abstract.

Note to self: remember these stories when the design meetings get bogged down in lane widths and MUTCD citations. This is about making sure my kids, my parents, and my neighbors can get through a day without ending up in an ER—or a courtroom.

metadata.title: Why 14th Ave and 62nd St won’t let me go

artifacts.content:
Event: Stockton Blvd / 14th Ave site walk + Mom’s chemo

Time: Thursday, 3/6/2025

1:00–2:30 PM – Field review along Stockton Blvd and connecting segments (check that proposed work really stays within existing ROW – no new lanes, no surprises). Quick notes: fully urbanized corridor, no obvious biological resources, just the usual cracked sidewalks and faded crosswalks.

2:30–3:00 PM – Drive from office to Mom’s place in South Sac.

3:00–6:00 PM – Take Mom (Rosa) to UC Davis Comprehensive Cancer Center for stage III breast cancer chemo infusion.

Reminders:
- Bring camera and notebook for Stockton Blvd, but don’t overdo it so I’m not late picking up Mom.
- Ask nurse about her fatigue and whether the last round’s dizziness is normal.
- Double‑check with Mom about her 14th Ave walking schedule to Kaiser South Sac (30–40 min, three times a week) and whether any crossings feel unsafe so I can keep those in mind for future lighting/curb ramp work.

Context for me: still feeling the weight of the 2019 Broadway audit/PIP and the October 2024 whistleblower grievance. I need to keep my Class 1 write‑ups bulletproof—no missed hazmat flags, no sloppy field notes—while also remembering that this afternoon is about being a son first, planner second.

Also: text Elena from the infusion room to see if she needs me to grab anything from Food Source on 14th & Stockton on the way home, since we’re still juggling one car and her clinic shifts.

metadata.title: 3/6/25 – Site walk + Mom chemo
metadata.location: Stockton Blvd corridor / UC Davis Comprehensive Cancer Center

artifacts.content:
Hi Maria,

Thanks again for talking me through the union stuff last month. Quick update and also a favor.

I met with Mark Ellison on 1/17/25 at 915 I Street about the latest Class 1 exemption. I told him I’m basically treating every hazmat check like the Broadway restriping do‑over, so I don’t repeat the 2019 audit mess that stuck that PIP in my file until June 2020. He nodded, but you know how it is—once something’s in the HR folder, it lives there.

On top of that, HR finally confirmed what I already suspected: the “anonymous” August 2023 complaint about me rubber‑stamping CEQA exemptions while I was on FMLA leave was from Kevin Liu. The part that really stung was them dragging in the fact that I was out for stress and anxiety after Mateo’s behavioral suspension from Mark Twain Elementary. It felt like my kid’s rough patch and my mental health were suddenly evidence.

The silver lining is that the whistleblower retaliation grievance I filed in October 2024 is at least being taken seriously. I met with City Attorney Lisa Hernandez on 12/12/24 about the former deputy director pressuring me to “find a way” around an EIR on that infill project. After that meeting I went straight to my 3rd‑floor cubicle and ran EnviroStor on every parcel for the new Class 1 jobs, just to have a clean paper trail.

Favor: can we schedule another check‑in about how all this interacts with my personnel record and FMLA history? I don’t want to overreact, but I also don’t want to be naive.

Appreciate you,
Daniel

Daniel: You free Friday night or are you still buried at the clinic?

Elena: Off at 6, so yes. What’s up?

Daniel: Was thinking low‑key movie night with the kids. Also kind of want to mark the fact that my license has been back for a while and we’ve made it through both of our DUI chapters without killing each other.

Elena: Dark joke but true. I still think about that March 2023 night at 14th & Stockton and the six‑month suspension. Walking up 14th to Food Source and the clinic with the kids in the heat was…humbling.

Daniel: Same. Every time I coordinate with Sac PD now, I flash back to my own May 2019 arrest near 21st Ave and the 18‑month DUI program. When I talked to them on Feb 3 about scheduling night work around their checkpoint near 21st, I didn’t mention that’s exactly where I got popped.

Elena: Maybe don’t lead with that in coordination meetings.

Daniel: Haha, yeah. But honestly, those experiences are part of why I’m so obsessive about keeping the projects by the book—Class 1 rehab only, no funny business—and why I care so much about safe walking routes. We’ve both had seasons where we *had* to walk.

Elena: True. Ok, Friday: simple pasta, kids’ choice movie, no work talk after 8.

Daniel: Deal. I’ll bike home unless it’s dumping rain, then I’ll actually use our one car responsibly.

Elena: Growth. 😄

From: Daniel Morales <dmorales@sacpublicworks.gov>
To: Elena Ruiz Morales <elenaruiz@example.com>
Date: March 12, 2024 9:14 PM
Subject: Why these school projects feel so personal

Elena,

I’m finishing up the narrative for the Safe Routes and 14th Ave packages tonight and just needed to write this out to you. On paper it’s all “traffic-calming” and “CEQA Class 1 – Existing Facilities,” but in my head it’s Sofia, Mateo, and our block.

For the Safe Routes to School project on the 62nd Street corridor and Franklin Boulevard by Ethel I. Baker Elementary School, I keep going back to March 3, 2023. That near-collision at the crosswalk in front of Ethel I. Baker, when the speeding driver blew through and came within a few feet of hitting Sofia as she crossed with her classmates, is still burned into me. The curb extensions, new LED streetlights, and high‑visibility crosswalks we’re proposing along 62nd are really about protecting kids like ours who walk and bike there every weekday during the 2023–2024 school year—Sofia at 10 and Mateo at 7.

Same with the 14th Avenue Complete Streets and Safety Improvements Project. I framed it as a Class 1 exemption under CEQA Guidelines §15301(c) because it’s just resurfacing, restriping, lighting upgrades, and ADA‑compliant curb ramps within the existing right‑of‑way along 14th Avenue. But what I’m really thinking about is Sofia’s wheelchair‑accessible school bus struggling to safely load and unload near Oak Ridge Elementary since her March 2023 spinal surgery at UC Davis Medical Center.

On the forms I talk about “vulnerable users.” Privately, I’m just trying to keep our kids—and kids like them—safe.

– D

metadata.subject: Why these school projects feel so personal

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Morales <dmorales@sacpublicworks.gov>
To: 14th Ave Project File <14thAve_ProjectNotes@cityofsac.gov>
Date: January 29, 2024 7:02 AM
Subject: Personal background for internal context – 14th Ave / 62nd St (DO NOT DISTRIBUTE)

Team,

Adding some personal context here strictly for internal understanding of why I’ve been so adamant about the 14th Avenue and 62nd Street safety scope. Please do NOT include this verbatim in any public documents.

Along 14th Avenue near Oak Park and Tallac Village, the Complete Streets and Safety Improvements Project became urgent for me after two incidents at 14th Avenue and 58th Street. On the morning of September 14, 2023, my daughter, Sofia Morales, was nearly sideswiped in the crosswalk there—a near-miss sideswipe in the crosswalk by a speeding driver. Earlier, in May 2022, my neighbor’s son Diego was actually hit at that same location, a pedestrian collision with a speeding driver that ended with emergency medical treatment at UC Davis Medical Center. Those events are why I pushed within the City of Sacramento Department of Public Works to prioritize this 1.2‑mile segment so families in our Oak Park and Tallac Village neighborhoods, especially kids walking to Ethel I. Baker Elementary School and nearby churches, aren’t facing these kinds of close calls and injuries.

Separately, on the 62nd Street residential segment by my home at 4125 62nd Street, chronic speeding and cut‑through traffic have led to multiple near‑misses. In May 2022, my mother, Rosa Morales, was almost struck while unloading my son Mateo from our car in front of our single‑family residence frontage. The traffic‑calming elements we’re proposing—curb extensions and improved lighting at intersections near our block—are intended to protect elderly residents like her and families with young children who regularly park and cross there.

– Daniel Christopher Morales, Senior Planner, Environmental Services

metadata.subject: Personal background for internal context – 14th Ave / 62nd St (DO NOT DISTRIBUTE)

artifacts.content:
Personal planning note – not for public release
Author: Daniel Christopher Morales
Date: February 4, 2024
Location: Desk, 915 I Street

Purpose: Capture personal motivations for project narratives (use only as background when drafting grant/CEQA language).

14th Avenue Complete Streets and Safety Improvements Project (Oak Park / Tallac Village)
- Nature/purpose: Safety and access upgrades (resurfacing, restriping, lighting, ADA ramps) along 14th Avenue, Sacramento.
- Beneficiaries: School‑age kids and families traveling to Ethel I. Baker Elementary School, Oak Ridge Elementary, and neighborhood churches.
- Personal context: 
  • Near-miss sideswipe in crosswalk involving my daughter, Sofia Morales, at 14th Avenue and 58th Street, Sacramento, on the morning of September 14, 2023.
  • Pedestrian collision with speeding driver in May 2022 at same intersection involving neighbor’s son Diego; required emergency medical treatment at UC Davis Medical Center.
  • Since Sofia’s March 2023 spinal surgery at UC Davis Medical Center, her wheelchair‑accessible school bus has had difficulty safely loading/unloading near Oak Ridge Elementary; ADA‑compliant curb ramps in existing right‑of‑way directly address this.
  • CEQA: Exempt under Guidelines §15301(c) (Class 1 – Existing Facilities); emphasize no expansion of roadway capacity.

62nd Street corridor / residential segment (around 4125 62nd Street, Sacramento, CA 95820)
- Nature/purpose: Safe Routes to School / traffic‑calming to reduce speeding and cut‑through traffic along Franklin Boulevard and the 62nd Street corridor.
- Personal context: 
  • Near-collision at school crosswalk on March 3, 2023 in front of Ethel I. Baker Elementary School on Franklin Boulevard—driver failed to stop, came within a few feet from the vehicle hitting Sofia.
  • Chronic speeding on 62nd; May 2022 near‑miss while my mother, Rosa Morales, was unloading Mateo in front of our single‑family residence frontage.
  • Daily walk/bike commute to school every weekday during the 2023–2024 school year for Sofia (10) and Mateo (7).

metadata.title: Personal planning note – 14th Ave / 62nd St safety projects
